Part 1 of 2 — The Token Bucket, and the Single-Server Version That Works Beautifully (On One Server)
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Bucket4j rate limitingLet me tell you about the afternoon one client, all by itself, DDoSed us — and it wasn’t even trying to.
A customer’s integration had a retry loop with no backoff. Some downstream call started returning errors, so their code retried. The retry also failed, so it retried the retry, in a tight while loop, across their entire fleet, as fast as the network would carry it. From our side it looked like a coordinated attack: one API key, tens of thousands of requests a second, all hammering the same endpoint. Our thread pool filled. Our database connection pool drained. The requests from that one broken client crowded out the thousand honest clients, and the whole API tipped over — 500s for everybody, an incident channel on fire, and a root cause that was, humiliatingly, a customer's for loop.
Here’s the part that stuck with me. We had all the machinery to survive this — we just hadn’t put a doorman on it. The fix wasn’t more capacity or a faster database. It was the ability to look at that one client, notice it had asked for its ten-thousandth token in a second, and say a small, cheap, polite word — 429, Too Many Requests — to it, so we didn’t have to say a catastrophic 500, Internal…
